title: Information Security Analysts
jobTitles: Information Technology Security Analyst (IT Security Analyst)
keyDrivers: Security analysts face an ever-evolving landscape of threats where attackers constantly find novel ways to breach systems, making genuine human judgment in ambiguous, high-stakes situations essential. They spend enormous amounts of time on alert triage and log review that AI will compress, letting them focus on the harder investigative and strategic work. As AI expands the digital attack surface and creates new threat vectors, demand for skilled humans who can direct and validate AI-powered security tools will grow substantially.

taskData.full: Monitor current reports of computer viruses to determine when to update virus protection systems.
Develop plans to safeguard computer files against accidental or unauthorized modification, destruction, or disclosure and to meet emergency data processing needs.
Modify computer security files to incorporate new software, correct errors, or change individual access status.
Encrypt data transmissions and erect firewalls to conceal confidential information as it is being transmitted and to keep out tainted digital transfers.
Monitor use of data files and regulate access to safeguard information in computer files.
Confer with users to discuss issues such as computer data access needs, security violations, and programming changes.
Perform risk assessments and execute tests of data processing system to ensure functioning of data processing activities and security measures.
Review violations of computer security procedures and discuss procedures with violators to ensure violations are not repeated.
Document computer security and emergency measures policies, procedures, and tests.
Coordinate implementation of computer system plan with establishment personnel and outside vendors.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: Security policy documentation shows 41.2% automation in observed AI usage - the most direct signal that AI is handling a real slice of the written output work in this role [1]. The bigger structural risk is at the entry level: AI-assisted SIEM platforms and automated triage tools are absorbing tier-1 alert monitoring, which has historically been the on-ramp into the field [2].

opportunities.body: Risk assessment and encryption work shows strong augmentation patterns - 47.9% and 58.7% respectively - with thousands of observed conversations where analysts stay firmly in the loop [1]. The role scores a 5 out of 5 on directing AI tools, and every security breach carries regulatory and legal consequences that organizations cannot delegate to automated systems [2]. BLS projects 28% employment growth through 2034, driven by demand for analysts who can interpret AI-flagged threats and respond to novel attack patterns.
opportunities.statLabel: annual job openings, driven by expanding attack surfaces and regulatory requirements
opportunities.statSourceTitle: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Information Security Analysts

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start logging time against AI-assisted detection tools - specifically learning to configure and tune SIEM alert thresholds rather than just monitoring outputs. Over the next six months, build depth in incident response and threat hunting, the work that sits above automated triage and requires judgment under real operational pressure [2]. Analysts who can direct AI tools and own the investigation layer are pulling ahead of those whose value was in manual alert review [1].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The entry bar has shifted: Security+ certification plus hands-on lab work on TryHackMe or Hack The Box now signals more than a CS degree alone, because hiring managers want evidence of practical investigation skills, not just coursework. Build a portfolio that shows you can operate in realistic attack scenarios - document a mock incident response from detection through remediation. The talent shortage is real and the growth projections are among the strongest in tech, but the jobs available increasingly require intermediate skills, not starter-level monitoring [2].

quotes.quote: AI-powered SIEM tools have raised the floor on detection — but they've also raised the volume of alerts analysts have to triage. The skills that matter now are knowing what to investigate and what to suppress, and that requires deep familiarity with your environment, not just the tooling.

The rule generation and anomaly detection layers are getting automated quickly. What stays human is the threat modeling — understanding the attacker's logic well enough to find what the automated systems aren't tuned to catch. That's where security professionals need to invest their development time.
quotes.attribution: ACM Queue, 'Automation and Human Judgment in Security Operations'

quotes.quote: Security+ gets you in the door, but hiring managers now want to see lab work — TryHackMe, Hack The Box, a home lab with a SIEM. Candidates who can show they've done the hands-on work, not just passed the exam, are moving through our pipeline significantly faster.

CISA's AI roadmap identifies the ability to assess AI-enabled threats, secure AI systems, and use AI tools for cyber defense as priority competencies for the next generation of security professionals. Candidates who can speak to all three sides of that triangle — threat, defense, and tooling — will stand out in a field where AI literacy is becoming a baseline expectation.

sources.name: Anthropic / O*NET
sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index - Task-level automation and augmentation estimates for Information Security Analysts

sources.name: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics
sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Information Security Analysts

sources.name: ACM Queue
sources.title: ACM Queue: Practitioner Perspectives on AI in Software

careerCluster.title: Computer Science Researcher
careerCluster.fit: Your hands-on security work gives you real systems experience, but this is a step up that requires deeper theoretical and mathematical foundations, original research, and the ability to design systems from scratch rather than defend existing ones.
steps: Earn a master's or PhD in computer science
Publish or contribute to a security research paper
Build a novel tool solving an unsolved technical problem

careerCluster.title: Data Scientist
careerCluster.fit: Both roles involve working closely with data, access patterns, and system behavior, but security analysts focus on protecting data while data scientists focus on extracting meaning from it — making this a lateral move that requires building new statistical and programming skills.
steps: Learn Python and R for data analysis
Complete a machine learning cert (Coursera, DataCamp)
Build 2-3 portfolio projects using real datasets

careerCluster.title: Information Security Engineer
careerCluster.growth: Much faster than average (7% or higher)
careerCluster.fit: You already do the defensive groundwork — monitoring, access control, encryption — and this role is a step up into owning the engineering and architecture of those same systems at a deeper technical level.
steps: Earn CompTIA Security+ or CASP+ certification
Learn vulnerability scanning with Nessus or Qualys
Build a home lab: configure firewalls, run penetration tests

careerCluster.title: Information Technology Project Manager
careerCluster.growth: Much faster than average (7% or higher)
careerCluster.fit: You already coordinate with users, manage security plans, and track system changes, but this move is lateral — shifting from owning technical outcomes to owning schedules, budgets, and people coordination across IT projects.
steps: Earn PMP or CAPM certification
Lead an internal IT security project end-to-end
Learn MS Project or Jira for project tracking

careerCluster.title: Computer System Architect
careerCluster.growth: Much faster than average (7% or higher)
careerCluster.fit: Your background in securing systems and monitoring threats overlaps directly with systems monitoring and maintenance, but the target role shifts focus from protecting systems to designing, building, and directing how entire systems are structured and deployed.
steps: Earn AWS or Azure Solutions Architect certification
Build a home lab deploying full network infrastructure
Shadow or assist on a systems design project at work

careerCluster.title: Software Developer
careerCluster.fit: An information security analyst moving to software development is a lateral move — both roles require programming and systems thinking, but development shifts focus from protecting systems to building them.
steps: No new credential required
Complete backend or full-stack project portfolio
Earn AWS Certified Developer or similar (optional)

careerCluster.title: Database Architect
careerCluster.fit: An information security analyst moving to database architecture is a lateral move — your deep familiarity with data access control and system design carries over, but the job shifts from protecting data to designing how it's structured and stored.
steps: Earn Oracle Database Architect or Microsoft Azure Data certification
Build portfolio of database schema designs
Learn SQL Server, PostgreSQL, or NoSQL at depth

careerCluster.title: Software Quality Assurance Analyst
careerCluster.fit: An information security analyst moving to QA is a step down in seniority — your eye for how systems fail and your documentation habits carry over, but the role narrows from securing systems to testing software functionality.
steps: No new credential required
Learn bug tracking tools: JIRA, TestRail
Build test plan samples and defect reports for portfolio

careerCluster.title: Web Designer
careerCluster.fit: An information security analyst moving to web and digital interface design is a step down in technical scope and a significant domain shift — the collaboration and client-communication experience carries over, but the work moves from hardening systems to designing user experiences.
steps: No new credential required
Complete UX/UI design bootcamp or Google UX Design Certificate
Build a design portfolio in Figma

careerCluster.title: Web Administrator
careerCluster.fit: An information security analyst moving to web administration is a step down — your intrusion monitoring and vulnerability knowledge is directly applicable to web admin security tasks, but the overall scope and compensation are lower.
steps: No new credential required
Familiarize with CMS platforms: WordPress, Drupal
Apply existing security monitoring skills to web server context

careerCluster.title: Web Developer
careerCluster.fit: An information security analyst moving to web development is a step down in seniority — programming knowledge transfers, but the role shifts from hardening systems to building web applications, with narrower security focus.
steps: No new credential required
Build 2-3 web app portfolio projects
Learn a frontend framework: React or Vue

emergingCareers.title: AI Red Team Analyst
emergingCareers.description: Probes AI systems for vulnerabilities, adversarial inputs, and failure modes before deployment. Works with security and ML teams to define threat models for LLM-powered products, run jailbreak and prompt injection tests, and document exploitable weaknesses in AI pipelines.

stat.text: AI red-teaming is now a required practice before deployment at major AI labs, with the White House executive order on AI mandating red-team testing for frontier models — creating dedicated roles at companies deploying AI systems.
stat.sourceTitle: Executive Order on the Safe, Secure, and Trustworthy Development and Use of AI

emergingCareers.search_query: "AI red team" OR "LLM security" analyst jobs 2025
emergingCareers.fit: Your threat modeling and penetration testing instincts transfer directly — the shift is from attacking infrastructure to attacking model behavior, prompt logic, and AI pipeline trust boundaries.
steps: Complete OWASP's LLM Top 10 guide and run Garak against a public LLM API to document at least 3 exploitable weaknesses
Study Microsoft's PyRIT framework and replicate one published jailbreak attack from a recent AI security paper
Pursue the GIAC AI Security Fundamentals (GAISC) certification or complete Stanford's AI Security short course on Coursera

emergingCareers.title: Security AI Engineer
emergingCareers.description: Builds and deploys AI-powered security tools including anomaly detection systems, automated threat intelligence pipelines, and LLM-assisted incident response tooling. Sits at the intersection of ML engineering and security operations, owning the full lifecycle from data pipeline to production alert.
emergingCareers.core_tools: Splunk SIEM, LangChain, AWS Security Hub

stat.text: The cybersecurity workforce gap reached 4 million unfilled positions globally in 2024, with AI and automation skills listed as the fastest-growing requirement in security job postings.

emergingCareers.search_query: "security AI engineer" OR "AI-powered security" engineer jobs 2025
emergingCareers.fit: Your understanding of security data — logs, alerts, network traffic — gives you a domain advantage when training anomaly detection models; the new skill is wiring ML outputs into actionable security workflows.
steps: Complete AWS Security Specialty certification to understand cloud security data sources that feed ML pipelines
Build an anomaly detection model on a public network log dataset (CICIDS or KDD Cup) and deploy it as a Splunk alert
Learn LangChain basics and build a prototype LLM-powered triage assistant that classifies incoming security alerts
